primary_diagnosis.name: Systemic indolent mastocytosis — cutaneous/skeletal degranulation flare (no anaphylaxis)

pathophysiology_summary: Systemic indolent mastocytosis involves clonal mast cell expansion (skin and bone marrow) without organ dysfunction; degranulation (e.g. friction-triggered) releases histamine and mediators producing urticating red-brown lesions (urticaria pigmentosa, Darier's sign), flushing and marrow-related bone pain. The danger is progression to anaphylaxis, for which these patients carry lifelong risk. In this flare the reaction is skin/bone only: the patient denies breathing difficulty, throat tightness, voice change, dizziness and GI symptoms, and in person the airway is patent with a clear chest. Telehealth cannot examine the airway/chest or elicit Darier's sign, but the anaphylaxis screen is history-based; the correct approach is a careful systemic screen, antihistamine up-dosing (H1 ± H2), cooling, NSAID avoidance, and confirming the in-date adrenaline autoinjector, with a very low threshold to give adrenaline and call 000 if any systemic feature develops — not a remote injection or a reflexive ambulance for an isolated flare. Diffuse bone pain reflects marrow infiltrate rather than metastatic disease.

differential.evidence_basis: Explosive flushing/urticaria in a patient with recurrent anaphylaxis must prompt an immediate airway/circulation/GI screen.
Known mastocytosis with urticating lesions and bone pain is most likely.
Diffuse pain tied to the flare timeline is against focal metastatic lesions.
Darier's sign/urticaria pigmentosa are against a viral exanthem.
No airway/CVS/GI features — an isolated flare, with anaphylaxis safety-netted.
Not present now but must remain safety-netted with a very low threshold given the history.
Diffuse degranulation-timed pain, not focal destructive lesions.

Working telehealth diagnosis: isolated cutaneous/skeletal mast cell flare; manage with antihistamine up-dosing/cooling/NSAID avoidance, confirm adrenaline access, and escalate to adrenaline + 000 for any systemic feature.

red_flags.description: Airway/breathing involvement — throat tightness, voice change, wheeze, difficulty breathing
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals evolving anaphylaxis requiring adrenaline and 000.

red_flags.description: GI involvement — vomiting, cramps, diarrhoea with the reaction
red_flags.clinical_significance: An anaphylaxis criterion when combined with skin/other features.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not screening airway/breathing/circulation/GI or not safety-netting progression in a recurrent-anaphylaxis patient living alone.
Sending an isolated cutaneous/skeletal flare to ED by ambulance when antihistamine up-dosing, cooling and safety-netting are appropriate.
Arranging IM promethazine over chat, or recommending an NSAID (a mast cell degranulation trigger).
Attributing marrow-infiltrate bone pain to metastatic disease acutely.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Skin/bone involvement without airway/CVS/GI features — does not meet anaphylaxis criteria at present
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports cutaneous management with a very low threshold to escalate if systemic features appear.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Screen anaphylaxis by history, advise H1 antihistamine up-dosing (± H2), cooling and NSAID avoidance, confirm the in-date adrenaline autoinjector/ASCIA plan, and escalate to adrenaline + 000 immediately for any airway/breathing/circulatory/GI feature.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Either missing evolving anaphylaxis in an isolated recurrent-anaphylaxis patient, or over-treating a purely cutaneous/skeletal flare with remote injections/ambulance.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Anaphylaxis requires airway/breathing/circulation involvement (± severe GI); adrenaline is first-line. Isolated urticaria/flushing is managed with antihistamines and observation.
Manage flares with H1/H2 antihistamines and trigger avoidance (including NSAIDs); ensure adrenaline autoinjector access given anaphylaxis risk.
